# Questions in Livestream 253

Live Stream #253: The Grassy Nome Theory

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=556" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:16</a> | Is it sinful to abuse the `weaker brother` idea by trying to hold other people's private behavior hostage? | Yes. He said flaunting freedom can be sinful in public, but using that text to control someone else's private conduct is also a misuse of the passage. | He distinguished between public conduct that may make a weaker brother stumble and private conduct that is not the weaker brother's business. The passage is about public settings such as eating or drinking in front of others, so repeatedly invoking it to pressure someone else's private behavior takes the text out of context and fails to love the brother. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=697" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:37</a> | Is there evidence for a 40-year desolation of Egypt under Nebuchadnezzar in Ezekiel 29? | He said there is some evidence of Babylonian looting and disruption in Egypt's heartland, but not strong evidence for a literal total desolation. | He read Ezekiel's language as hyperbolic and limited the geography to Egypt's heartland, roughly from Migdol to Syene and toward Kush, rather than all Egypt. He connected the Babylonian pursuit of fleeing Judeans with looting, disrupted government, and ransacked temples, but stressed that the evidence is limited and not a strong proof of a literal empty-land desolation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=930" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:30</a> | Who was the woman scholar mentioned for prehistoric Anatolia? | He clarified that he meant Erica Hughes as a specialist in prehistoric Anatolia, not an antediluvian or pre-flood scholar. | He said the earlier remark was about prehistoric Anatolia, modern Turkey, not the pre-flood world. He identified Erica Hughes as an Anatolian or Hittite specialist, described her as knowledgeable in Anatolian prehistory, and gave examples such as Gobekli Tepe and prehistoric agriculture while cautioning that she does not have many publications. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=1030" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:10</a> | Why do some early-date Exodus proponents say the previous pharaoh must have reigned more than 40 years? | He said the argument depends on combining Acts 7 with Exodus 4, but he rejected it because Exodus 4:19 says all the men seeking Moses' life were dead, not one pharaoh. | He steelmanned the claim from Acts 7:30, Exodus 2:23, and Exodus 4:19: Moses was gone 40 years, a king died, and therefore the prior pharaoh must have reigned more than 40 years. His critique was that the argument depends on misquoting Exodus 4:19 as singular, whereas the Hebrew, translations, Septuagint, and Samaritan Pentateuch refer to all the men seeking Moses' life, which could include courtiers, generals, scribes, or relatives of the slain Egyptian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=1648" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:28</a> | Who were the `Philistines` in Abraham and Isaac's time? | He treated that label as a retrojection for later readers and thought the people in view were Canaanites on the coastal plain, not actual later Philistines or `Paleo-Hyksos`. | He argued that the Pentateuch is using a later geographic label so Israelite readers would understand the coastal-plain setting. The people themselves were not the later Philistines and were not Paleo-Hyksos, because he associated the latter with Amorites and did not think Amorites reached the coastal plain in view; he identified the group as Canaanite. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=1745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:05</a> | Why do the borders in Genesis 15 seem much larger than later Israelite territory? | He said the promise is to Abraham's descendants broadly, not only to Israel, so it includes other related peoples spread across a much wider region. | He emphasized that Genesis 15 speaks of Abraham's descendants, not only Israelites. That broader category includes groups such as Moabites, Ishmaelites, Kenites, and other related peoples, some moving north toward Edom, Moab, and Aram and others ranging through Sinai, so the promised swath can be broader than later Israelite territory. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=1890" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:30</a> | Did Solomon's kingdom extend to most of the Genesis 15 territory? | Not most of it. He said Solomon's kingdom reached up toward the Golan Heights and a little into Syria, but did not cover the whole promised range. | He said Solomon's territory was large compared with David's but still limited. It reached toward the Golan Heights and somewhat into Syria, possibly with hegemonic control farther out, but did not extend to the Euphrates, did not control Gaza or most of the coastal plain, and only reached southward to Elath on the Gulf of Aqaba. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=1997" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:17</a> | What are your thoughts on the Kalašma language tablet? | He said only that it appears to reflect a Luwian dialect related to Hittite and did not claim detailed familiarity with it. | He said he had not looked deeply into the tablet. From what he understood, it begins by identifying the Kalašma language and appears to be a Luwian or Luwian-related spoken language within the Hittite sphere, written in cuneiform rather than hieroglyphic Luwian, but he limited his answer to that broad impression. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=2056" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:16</a> | What are your thoughts on the Philistine language? | He said he did not know enough to give an opinion. | He was explicit that he had not looked into the Philistine language and did not know what inscriptions, if any, had been found. He treated it as an interesting question, but said he would need to research it before saying more. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=2135" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:35</a> | May Christians eat foods such as pork and shrimp? | Yes. He treated the dietary restrictions as part of the fulfilled ritual law rather than binding on Christians. | He placed kosher food restrictions under the ritual law and said Christ fulfilled that law. Because the ritual law is fulfilled, Christians are not obligated to keep the dietary regulations as binding covenant practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=2304" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:24</a> | What do you think of Graeme Hancock, Mark Armitage, and `big archaeology` conspiracy claims? | He rejected the conspiracy framing, arguing archaeology is underfunded and that such claims confuse fields and ignore the actual evidence problems. | He said there is no meaningful `big archaeology` suppressing discoveries, noting that many archaeologists barely make money and often pay heavily for publication and fieldwork. He also pointed out that dinosaur bones belong to paleontology, not archaeology, and treated claims about grants, universities, or companies paying critics as conspiracy thinking that avoids evidence problems such as fake finds or unsupported claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=2843" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:23</a> | What happens to Christians who die before Christ's return? | He said believers go directly to be with the Lord in heaven before the final resurrection. | He appealed to being absent from the body and present with the Lord, Revelation's picture of saints around the throne, and Jesus' promise to the thief about paradise. On that basis he said the dead in Christ go directly to heaven, not to Sheol or Hades as a stopover. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=2940" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:00</a> | Do Christians pray in heaven and in the new heavens and new earth? | He said prayer as a means of conforming our will becomes unnecessary in heaven because there is direct access to God. | He defined prayer chiefly as conforming one's will to God's will, with communication also involved. In heaven that conforming function is redundant because believers are transformed, and communication does not require prayer because they can speak directly; in the new heavens and earth he compared access to God to Adam walking with God in Eden. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=3106" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:46</a> | In the new heavens and earth, who gets scarce time with Jesus? | He said those who stored up treasure in heaven would have the most time with Christ. | He distinguished access to God generally from time with the embodied Christ. Believers can still communicate with the Father and the Spirit, but he described direct time with Jesus as a scarce resource tied to storing up treasure in heaven. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=3203" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:23</a> | Will believers be able to know that the Father or Spirit are present? | Yes. He said believers would know by divine glory. | He compared future access to Adam knowing God was present in Eden. He also identified the Holy Spirit with the Shekinah glory and said that emanating glory would make God's presence knowable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=3230" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:50</a> | Will people need to cover their eyes before God like angels around the throne? | He said probably not, treating the angelic wing-covering more as modesty language than as a response to personal sinfulness. | He said he did not think believers would need to cover their eyes because sin will no longer be in them. The angelic covering is not because angels have sin, but because the imagery is about modesty before God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=3279" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:39</a> | Did Jesus permanently set aside omnipresence to save us? | He corrected the language to multipresence and said Jesus did not set it aside, though his human body remains in one place. | He said none of the Trinity should be described as omnipresent in that sense, preferring multipresent. In his explanation, Christ remains fully God and fully man, so the spirit of Christ can be multipresent while the embodied human body is unipresent and not mixed with divine attributes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=3399" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:39</a> | Will believers have wings, fly, or levitate in heaven? | He did not think believers would get wings or the ability to fly. | The answer was brief and playful, but direct. He responded by asking whether people can fly now, then said he did not think believers would get wings or flight in the afterlife. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=3439" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:19</a> | Do Jewish Christians still need to keep the ritual law? | No. He said the ritual law is fulfilled in Christ, even if some Jewish believers still choose to observe parts of it. | He said Jewish Christians do not have to keep the ritual law because it too is fulfilled in Christ. They may choose to observe covenant customs such as Passover, booths, or parts of Yom Kippur, but those practices are not necessary or binding as ritual law. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=3535" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:55</a> | Have any Revelation events happened in this century? | He said not so far. | His answer was brief and cautious: the twenty-first century was still young, and he did not think Revelation events had occurred in it so far. He allowed that one might discuss the previous century differently, but for this century he answered no. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=3581" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:41</a> | Will pets and other animals go to heaven? | He tentatively thought some animals do, while qualifying that not all animals necessarily share the same destiny or relationship to humans there. | He qualified the answer by saying he does not think all animals or all pets necessarily go to heaven in the same way. He suggested animals in relationship with humans may develop moral reasoning and something worth preserving, while animals outside that relationship might have their own afterlife rather than necessarily being with humans. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=3764" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:44</a> | Will there be a Dr. Falk recommended reading list? | Probably not soon, though he said an old spirit-world reading list exists somewhere on the community tab. | He said he does not usually collate reading lists because he reads too much across too many books for that to be easy. Apart from an older spirit-world list on the community tab, he did not expect to produce another list anytime soon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=3835" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:55</a> | Is the story in 1 Kings 3:16-28 original to Kings? | He said it is original to Kings, while the harder question is whether it goes back to Solomon himself; he leaned toward yes because it fits Solomon's reputation for wisdom. | He distinguished between originality to Kings and historicity from Solomon's life. Since Kings is a post-exilic compilation from earlier materials, the story is original to Kings as a book, while the harder question is whether it preserves a Solomon-era event or an illustrative wisdom story; he leaned toward a Solomonic origin because it fits Solomon's reputation for wisdom and human insight. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=3992" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:32</a> | Would anyone actually react by saying to divide the child in Solomon's judgment? | Yes. He said the reaction is consistent with human spite and shows which claimant does not truly care for the child. | He said an ancient king could plausibly order the child killed with impunity, so the test would expose the claimant who did not truly care. The willingness to divide the child reveals spite rather than motherhood, and he compared that motive to modern disputes where a child may be used to wound another person. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=4131" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:51</a> | Do we know anything about the wise men named in 1 Kings 4:31? | Not really. He said they seem to have been known wisdom figures around Solomon's time, but no independent records of them are known to him. | He read the names as examples of known wisdom figures who were either contemporary with Solomon or known leading up to his time. He did not know of surviving independent records for them, while leaving open that evidence could be found later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=4255" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:55</a> | In 1 Kings 6:18, was it important that no stone could be seen in the temple? | He said the stone was paneled with cedar, carved, and overlaid with gold, likely to achieve ornate relief work the local stone could not provide well. | He explained that Solomon's builders used a stone structure but covered the interior with close-fitting cedar panels, carved the cedar with ornamental designs, and overlaid it with gold. Since Jerusalem limestone was not as suitable for fine relief as Egyptian stone, wood paneling allowed ornate temple decoration and the absence of visible stone showed tight, skilled workmanship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=4411" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:31</a> | Will a third temple be built, and must the Dome of the Rock fall first? | He thought a third temple will be built, but said the Dome of the Rock need not be removed because he locates the relevant temple site near the Dome of the Tablets. | He said the Holy of Holies needs to stand over a small rise in the bedrock, not the much larger exposed rock under the Dome of the Rock. He placed the relevant rock near the Dome of the Tablets, far enough away that an east-west temple could be built without removing the Dome of the Rock. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=4672" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:52</a> | In 1 Kings 6:20, was it acceptable for Solomon to alter the altar? | Yes. He said cedar and gold are ritually pure, and Solomon needed new temple furnishings anyway. | He saw no ritual problem with overlaying or altering the altar because cedar and gold are ritually pure materials. He added that Solomon had to make new temple furnishings in any case because the older altar would have been too small for the temple context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=4740" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:00</a> | Does 1 Kings 8:8 show that this section was written before the exile? | He said it may preserve a pre-exilic source, but Kings as a compiled book was collated during the exile from earlier materials. | He said wording such as `to this day` can reflect pre-exilic source material copied into Kings. His larger model is that exilic compilers combined earlier books and records, so pre-exilic remnants can appear in a book whose final collation happened during the exile. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=4847" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:47</a> | Was Pharaoh's daughter Solomon's great queen? | Not as far as he knows. He said Israelite succession was not organized around a great-queen system the way Egyptian succession could be. | He said Israelite royal succession was based more on age than on the status of a great queen, unlike Egyptian succession where a great queen's son could have priority. Pharaoh's daughter may have had a separate house because she brought her own servants, wealth, and religious practices, but that does not make her a formal great queen in Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=5030" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:50</a> | How common was queen-mother authority, and does it translate to Mary in Christ's kingdom? | Queen mothers commonly had some authority, but he warned against using that political pattern to give Mary authority over Christ. | He said queen mothers commonly had some recognized authority across kingdoms and periods, but the analogy becomes dangerous when applied to Mary and Christ. Mary can be honored as a faithful servant, yet using a human court pattern to give her authority over or equal to Christ would, in his view, cross a serious theological line. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=5246" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:26</a> | Is Matthew 12:48-50 relevant to claims about Mary's authority? | Yes. He said Jesus' statement about his mother and brothers shows obedience to the Father, not biological relation, as the controlling issue. | He connected the passage to the claim about Mary's influence because Jesus identifies those who do the Father's will as his brother, sister, and mother. In his reading, that means biological relation to Jesus does not establish controlling spiritual authority. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=5365" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:25</a> | Does 1 Kings show that Egypt, Moses, and the Exodus were already cemented in Israel's memory? | Yes. He said Kings and the prophets already treat the Exodus as central to Israel's national identity, against claims that the Pentateuch came only later. | He rejected the idea that the prophets came before a developed Pentateuchal memory of Egypt and Moses. In his view, Kings, the histories, and prophets such as Isaiah and Ezekiel already present the Exodus as Israel's origin story and national identity, with Egypt repeatedly embedded in the biblical narrative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=5450" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:50</a> | Why did Hiram pay Solomon in 1 Kings 9:14? | He treated it as a toll or tariff for access through Solomon's territory, especially for trade routes toward Egypt. | He described the payment as an upfront fee for free passage. Hiram needed to move resources such as cedar through Solomon's territory toward Egypt, and Solomon controlled the east-west trade corridor, so the gold functioned as a toll, transit tax, or tariff for unhindered trade. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=5603" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:23</a> | Do we know which pharaoh took Gezer in 1 Kings 9:16? | He said Siamun is the most likely pharaoh in view. | He identified Siamun, the second-to-last king of Dynasty 21, as the most likely candidate because he was contemporary with Solomon's early years. He added that Egypt was in a weakened state, so taking Gezer would not have required the kind of power Egypt displayed in earlier periods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=5716" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:16</a> | Does 1 Kings 9:21 prove the beginning of Kings was written before the Babylonian exile? | Not the whole book. He said it shows Kings is copying pre-exilic source material, even though the final compilation is post-exilic or exilic. | He distinguished citation from compilation. References that sound pre-exilic show that the compiler copied earlier source documents, often verbatim, but they do not prove the whole beginning of Kings was written before the exile because the final book was compiled from many sources during or after the exile. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=5904" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:24</a> | What is the Millo in 1 Kings 9:24? | He identified the Millo as a defensive wall and leaned toward Eilat Mazar's case for locating it near the City of David palace area. | He described the Millo as a defensive wall, while noting uncertainty about its exact location. He mentioned the traditional northern defensive-wall identification but leaned toward Eilat Mazar's argument for a wall near Solomon's palace in the City of David area. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=6059" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:59</a> | Did Samson carry the city gate doors in Judges 16:3? | Yes, he thought the text implies Samson ripped off the doors with the posts and bars and carried them away. | He read Judges 16:3 as saying Samson took the city gate doors, posts, and bars and carried them to the mountain opposite Hebron. In response to weight concerns, he said ancient gates were defensive and therefore relatively narrow, not necessarily massive multi-ton structures, though Samson's unusual strength remains part of the point. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=6256" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:16</a> | Is the amount of gold in 1 Kings 10:14 possible? | Yes. He said ancient rulers accumulated large amounts of gold and compared the figure with the gold in Tutankhamun's tomb. | He estimated a talent around 60 to 80 pounds and treated 666 talents as a large but possible amount, especially given long accumulation of gold in ancient royal contexts. He compared it with Tutankhamun's tomb, where the innermost coffin alone was hundreds of pounds of gold and the full set of coffins and furnishings contained much more; the striking point for Solomon is that the text says the gold came in one year. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=6631" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:31</a> | How did Tutankhamun's tomb escape being fully raided before discovery? | He said it had been raided once, but a later work village covered the entrance and caused its location to be forgotten. | He said the tomb was not untouched, since evidence suggests it had been raided once. Its later preservation came because a workers' village, connected with later tomb construction, was placed over the entrance, blocking access until the location and even the chapel area were forgotten. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=6955" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:55</a> | Does Solomon's 666 talents imply high taxation? | No. He said that gold came through trade, tariffs, and transit, not necessarily through high taxation. | He tied the gold to Solomon's control of trade routes and the kind of toll or tariff arrangement discussed earlier with Hiram. In this answer, he rejected the idea that the 666 talents necessarily point to high taxation and instead framed it as revenue from commerce moving through the land. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=6982" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:22</a> | What do you think of the 616 manuscript reading in Revelation? | He said the 616 manuscript may be the oldest, but oldest is not automatically correct and the broader manuscript evidence favors 666. | He acknowledged that the 616 reading is in the oldest manuscript, but cautioned that the oldest witness is not always the correct one. He said the plurality of sources points away from 616 as the correct reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=7060" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:40</a> | How big would the lions on Solomon's throne have been? | The lions on the throne arms were probably modest, human-chair scale, while the lions on the steps could have been larger statuary. | He compared the throne to known royal chairs rather than oversized fantasy thrones. The arm lions were likely worked into a human-sized throne, while the twelve lions along the six steps could have been carved statues and therefore larger. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=7188" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:48</a> | Where is Kue in 1 Kings 10:28? | He guessed Cyprus, but said he was not sure. | He corrected the pronunciation away from `coup` and discussed Kue as a source connected with Solomon's horse imports. He suggested it could be Cyprus, while also mentioning Greek regions more broadly and qualifying the identification as uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=7310" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:50</a> | Could Manichaeism's similarities to Hinduism or Brahmanism indicate a connection? | He did not think Hinduism was the source; he remembered Manichaeism as Gnostic thought filtered through Zoroastrianism. | He qualified that his memory was hazy, but said Manichaeism seemed to come primarily from Gnostic ideas as they interacted with Zoroastrianism. He therefore did not treat Hinduism or Brahmanism as the likely source of the similarities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=7408" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:28</a> | How come Nimrod was not against God? | He said Nimrod was subduing wild land and beasts, which fits the creation mandate rather than opposing God. | He connected Nimrod's mighty hunting to the creation mandate to rule, subdue, and make the land fruitful. By killing wild beasts and making Mesopotamia safer for agriculture and human flourishing, Nimrod was doing the work of taming wild land rather than acting against God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=7576" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:16</a> | Was aversion to the material world common in Greco-Roman culture or unique to later Gnostic sects? | He said the idea has roots in Neoplatonism, but Gnostic religion drove it to a stronger anti-material conclusion. | He said the hierarchy that places spirit above matter has philosophical roots in Platonism and especially Neoplatonism. Gnostic sects made that religious and more extreme by treating the demiurge and the material world as deeply corrupt, a conclusion he did not describe as normal Greco-Roman life as a whole. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=7814" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:14</a> | Does Exodus 3:14 in the Septuagint connect with Plato's concept of the One? | He did not think so, treating the Septuagint wording as translation rather than a direct Platonic borrowing. | He said the Septuagint wording translates the Hebrew divine self-identification rather than intentionally invoking Plato's highest form. The translators may have known Platonic language, but he thought the phrasing was mainly a Greek rendering of `I am`, later developing toward `ego eimi` usage by the first century. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=8089" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:49</a> | Are Christians called to be nice, or is there a proper way to handle people who abuse Christian kindness? | He said Christians are called to be holy, truthful, and good, not merely nice. | He argued that niceness is not a biblical virtue in itself and can become a counterfeit for goodness, truthfulness, or moral courage. He said Christians should not excuse lies or evil merely to be pleasant, while still aiming at holiness and truth rather than cruelty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=8548" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:28</a> | Was Avarus the town of the Israelites, and were the Habiru the Hebrews? | He thought Avarus likely was connected with the Israelites, but said Habiru is a social class rather than an ethnic label. | He distinguished Israelite as an ethnonym from Habiru as a social status for outsiders, sojourners, mercenaries, robbers, or other marginal people. In his view, some Habiru could be Israelites and Abraham could be called Habiru as a sojourner, but the terms are not identical; Avarus, meanwhile, was an Amorite Hyksos capital where Israelites could have found a home. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=8831" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:11</a> | How do you determine the genre of a biblical text? | He said to read widely across ancient genres and attend to textual clues such as poetry, war chronicle language, prophetic material, apocalyptic imagery, or dedicatory inscriptions. | He said genre recognition requires exposure to many kinds of literature, either by reading widely or by using reference works on biblical genres. Then the text's own clues matter: war-chronicle hyperbole, poetic couplets, prophecy, apocalyptic imagery, temple dedication, execration texts, biography, and other patterns help identify how a passage should be read. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=9129" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:09</a> | Did Tertullian become and remain excommunicated? | He said Tertullian's excommunication is complicated and argued it happened before Montanism became the later explanation attached to it. | He argued that Tertullian was excommunicated before becoming associated with Montanism and that the reason was his position that priests could marry, together with his own marriage and refusal to divorce. He said Montanism later became the common explanation, but at the time it was not yet the same developed heretical movement, and Tertullian remained connected locally through figures such as Cyprian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=9569" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:29</a> | Could a settler population that replaces a native population change the cosmic ruler or prince of a country? | Possibly. He said either the cosmic ruler changes or switches to the new population, but the criteria are not clear enough to say more. | He allowed the possibility but refused to be precise. Either the cosmic ruler could change or the existing ruler could shift to the new population, but he said Scripture or available criteria do not tell us enough about how cosmic-ruler supremacy changes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=9779" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:59</a> | Where do you think the Garden of Eden was? | He located it in the ancient Sealand area near the Mesopotamian river mouths, now largely underwater. | He reasoned from the four rivers, identifying the Tigris and Euphrates and placing the other river geography near Mesopotamia's old coastline. Because the coastline shifted and the marshy Sealand area at the river mouths was largely covered by the sea, he thought Eden would now be underwater in that ancient Sealand region. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=9932" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:32</a> | When did ancient Israelites name their children? | He said the naming was generally done on the eighth day, tied to circumcision for boys and a naming rite for girls. | He described an eighth-day naming event in Israelite culture. Boys were circumcised then, girls received a water rite, and he connected the timing with infant survival after birth, saying it was not practical to name a baby immediately if survival was still uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=10061" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:41</a> | What is the context of `blessed are the peacemakers` in Matthew 5:9? | He said peacemakers defuse conflict and prefer peace; they do not force peace on opponents. | He read the Beatitudes as proverbial maxims rather than absolute rules for every case. Peacemaking means preventing unnecessary escalation and preferring peace, but it does not mean coercing peace or pretending that every conflict can or should be settled without confrontation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=10379" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:59</a> | Can you elaborate on being poor in spirit? | He described it as not flaunting spiritual status or strength, but approaching God with humility and dependence. | In this answer he focused especially on wealth and public posture: being poor in spirit does not mean having no money or that wealth is sinful, but refusing conspicuous displays of wealth and showing solidarity with the poor. He summarized the principle as earning and saving rightly while giving generously, with giving ranked above displaying wealth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=10565" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:05</a> | How do you interpret 1 Corinthians 14:15, and is it related to hair and reproduction beliefs? | He said no; he treated the passage as about modesty and ordered worship rather than a reproduction theory of hair. | He corrected the reference to 1 Corinthians 11:14-15 and said the hair issue is not about reproduction. He read it as a Corinthian cultural practice about humility in public worship, where hair or head coverings communicated modesty, and he applied the principle as honoring whatever cultural signals appropriately avoid pride or conspicuous display. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=10996" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:16</a> | Did ancient Israelites or Second Temple Jews know of a third power in heaven, the Holy Spirit? | Yes. He said they knew of the Holy Spirit as a third power, though later doctrine explains that more fully. | He said ancient Israelites and Second Temple Jews knew of the Father as Yahweh and also recognized a second-power pattern that could refer either to the Son or to the Shekinah glory, the Holy Spirit. They knew the Son and Spirit categories, but often conflated them as one second power rather than distinguishing a fully developed three-power or Trinitarian framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=11150" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:50</a> | Is it a fallacy to take first attestation as the beginning of existence? | Yes. He said first attestation only marks the earliest surviving evidence, not the actual beginning of a place, people, or practice. | He used Tell el-Retaba and Pithom as an example: a site may exist earlier under another name, while the name Pithom appears later after renaming tied to Ramesses II and the temple of Atum. He contrasted first attestation with actual foundation evidence, noting that a name's earliest evidence does not by itself prove when the place began. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=11356" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:16</a> | What does 1 Corinthians 7:14 mean about an unbelieving spouse and children being sanctified? | He said it describes a marriage where one spouse becomes a believer; the household gains access to the church community, not automatic salvation. | He framed the passage as a case where two unbelievers marry and one later becomes Christian. The unbelieving spouse and children are sanctified in the sense that the family can remain together and the children may be brought into the congregation, but he stressed that this does not make the spouse or children saved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=11522" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:02</a> | Have you visited the Israel Museum, and what are your thoughts? | No. He said he has never been to Israel or visited the museum, though he is familiar with some of its holdings. | He said he has never been to Israel or the Israel Museum, despite once being invited as the planned keynote speaker for a conference on the ark of the covenant. He knew the museum is in Jerusalem and is familiar with some holdings, but his answer stayed limited because he has not personally visited. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=11690" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:50</a> | Was Tertullian a bad guy or a heretic? | No. He said Tertullian was a faithful believer, a good teacher, and not a heretic. | He rejected the idea that Tertullian should be dismissed as a bad man or heretic. He described him as a faithful believer in Christ, a major intellect, and someone who believed the Holy Spirit continued speaking through the church. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=11862" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:42</a> | Are people afraid that getting theology wrong will send them to hell? | He said some errors can be serious, but many things labeled heresy do not exclude someone from salvation. | He distinguished ordinary theological error from heresies that change who God is or the status of humanity. In his view, some labels of heresy have been used too loosely, and the belief that the Holy Spirit continues to work through the church should not be treated as damnable error. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=12104" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:44</a> | Are you familiar with temple scholars Joseph Good and Rico Cortes? | No. He said they are outside his usual sphere of study and influence. | He said he is mainly an ancient Near East, Old Testament, and Egyptology scholar whose research intersects especially with the Pentateuch, the ark, the tabernacle, and Egyptian archaeology. Joseph Good and Rico Cortes are outside the modern temple-scholar material he normally interacts with, so he did not know their claims well enough to assess them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=12181" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:01</a> | Did the Assyrian Church of the East actually adhere to Nestorianism and heresy? | He said many in the Church of the East did embrace Nestorianism, and he treated it as non-Chalcedonian and not a recommended theology. | He described Nestorianism as teaching two persons in Christ, a divine or spirit person and a human person, rather than one person with two natures. He contrasted that with terminology problems in some other eastern churches and said the Church of the East was genuinely non-Chalcedonian in this respect. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/_6WmbdDHC8s?t=12376" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:26:16</a> | How should Protestants understand Mary as `Mother of God`? | Properly defined, he said it means Mary is mother of the incarnate second person of the Trinity, not mother of the Trinity as a whole. | He said most Protestant churches can accept Theotokos when it is defined as Mary bearing the incarnate God-man, Jesus Christ. The problem comes when people confuse the second person of the Trinity with God in totality; treating Mary as mother of the Trinity would be a category error and would lead into theological trouble. |
